title: Senate Russia Sanctions Bill Targets Dollar Dominance, Canada Hit With 50% Tariffs, and Oil Passes $91
subhead: The Graham Sanctioning Russia Act reaches 60+ Senate co-sponsors with tariffs up to 100% on Russian energy buyers, even as the White House worries about dollar erosion. Trump deploys an untested 1930s trade law to hit Canada with 50% tariffs. Oil climbs to a five-week high as the US-Iran conflict enters its 11th night and Houthis threaten Saudi shipping.

sections.heading: Russia Sanctions Bill Reaches Filibuster-Proof Majority — With a Dollar Problem
sections.body:
A bipartisan group of US senators introduced the Lindsey O. Graham Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026 on July 16, and by July 22 it had gathered more than 60 co-sponsors — enough to overcome a Senate filibuster. The bill, named after the late Senator Lindsey Graham who helped negotiate it days before his death, represents the strongest congressional effort yet to squeeze Moscow's war finances.

The revised legislation significantly reshapes an earlier draft. Rather than blanket 500% tariffs on countries buying Russian energy, the new version authorizes tariffs of up to 100% on the top five purchasers of Russian oil and natural gas — a group that sponsors said includes China and India. It creates exemptions for countries importing less than 15% of Russia's annual gas exports, provided they are taking 'significant steps' to reduce dependence.

Within days, Senate Majority Leader John Thune threw his weight behind the bill, calling it a priority. The legislation mandates sanctions within 30 days against Putin, senior Russian leaders, state-owned enterprises, financial institutions, and expands targeting of Russia's 'shadow fleet' of reflagged oil tankers. It also removes language making sanctions contingent on Russia's participation in peace talks — making many penalties mandatory.

But the bill has an uncomfortable side effect. According to the New York Times, the Trump administration has sought to scale back the sanctions push, concerned that adversaries' development of workarounds to the dollar-based financial system could erode US dollar supremacy. A Moneycontrol analysis noted the same tension: the bill's sweeping secondary sanctions on dollar-denominated transactions risk accelerating the very dedollarization it aims to prevent. The compromise grants the president waiver authority, with congressional notification requirements — but the core dilemma remains.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first sanctions bill with a filibuster-proof majority that explicitly targets Russian energy buyers like China and India. If passed, it would mark a significant escalation in financial warfare — but it could also push those buyers further toward non-dollar settlement systems.

sections.heading: Trump Hits Canada With 50% Tariffs Using Untested 1930s Law
sections.body:
President Trump on Monday signed three proclamations under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, imposing 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods. The tariffs cover products previously protected under USMCA, including wine, cement, hockey sticks, and other manufactured goods — though energy, potash, critical minerals, and fish are exempted.

The White House fact sheet cited Canada's 'discriminatory treatment' of American autos, alcohol, and dairy. It noted that Canadian imports of US motor vehicles fell 22% ($5.6 billion) from April 2025 through March 2026, while all but two Canadian provinces halted the sale of US alcoholic beverages, cutting imports by 81% ($582 million). The administration also highlighted restrictive Canadian dairy tariff-rate quotas that favour EU cheese over US cheese.

Section 338 has never been used before to impose tariffs. Several Democratic lawmakers attempted to repeal the provision last year, warning that Trump could use it to destabilize the economy. The tariffs take effect 30 days after signing.

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said he and Trump agreed to 'intensify trade negotiations' — but Trump also threatened additional tariffs over wildfire smoke drifting into the US, calling it 'willful' pollution. Meanwhile, US and Mexican negotiators launched a third round of bilateral USMCA talks in Mexico City on Tuesday, with the US having declined to extend the agreement in its current form on July 1. The Canadian dollar weakened 0.15% to C$1.409 per USD following the tariff announcement.
sections.whyItMatters: The use of Section 338 — an untested 1930s provision — to impose 50% tariffs on America's second-largest trading partner opens a new legal and economic frontier. With USMCA not renewed and bilateral talks ongoing, North American supply chains face months of disruption uncertainty.

sections.heading: Houthis Target Saudi Red Sea Shipping as Gulf Conflict Widens
sections.body:
Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels announced a maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia on Monday, threatening to target ships carrying Saudi crude through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait — the southern gateway to the Red Sea through which roughly 12% of global trade normally passes.

The threat had immediate effect. Two oil tankers carrying Saudi crude to Asia reversed course in the Red Sea on Tuesday, according to Reuters. The Bab el-Mandeb strait had become a key alternative export route for Saudi oil as shipments through the Strait of Hormuz declined sharply following the collapse of the US-Iran ceasefire earlier in July.

According to AP, the Houthis declared the blockade in retaliation for a blockade on Yemen and a recent attack on Sanaa International Airport. A USA Today analysis noted the move could disrupt energy shipments and increase risk to global markets. A Reuters commentary warned that if the Houthis start attacking ships near Bab el-Mandeb, it could 'peel off US resources from the Iran operation and exacerbate oil shortages.'

The escalation means both major Middle Eastern shipping chokepoints — Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb — are now under active threat simultaneously, something that has not occurred in modern maritime history.
sections.whyItMatters: With both Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb under threat, global energy shipping has no safe alternative route out of the Persian Gulf region. Saudi crude is now effectively blocked from its two main export pathways, which could rapidly tighten global supply.

sections.heading: Oil Surges to Five-Week High as US-Iran Strikes Enter 11th Night
sections.body:
Brent crude settled at $91.01 on Tuesday — the first close above $90 in more than a month — and edged higher to $91.51 in early Wednesday trading. WTI crude rose to $84.64. The moves mark a roughly 16% weekly gain driven by the dual chokepoint threat and the 11th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran.

According to a CNBC report, the dollar index rose 0.2% to 101.17, tracking its fourth consecutive session of gains as oil-driven inflation expectations reinforced the Fed's hawkish stance. Iran attacked US facilities in Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan in response to the strikes, while Kuwait's military reported intercepting Iranian drones over its airspace.

US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the military campaign in Iran has cost $37.5 billion so far — nearly $8 billion more than the previous public estimate. A senior Iranian official told Reuters that Tehran had received a proposal from mediators for a 10-day ceasefire, though no agreement has been reached.

Gold climbed to a near two-week high as investors sought safe-haven assets ahead of next week's Fed meeting, with markets pricing a 17.6% chance of a July hike and a 68.1% chance of a September hike, according to CME FedWatch.
sections.whyItMatters: Brent above $91 with both Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb under threat creates a compounding supply risk that markets have never priced in modern history. The Fed rate-hike expectations embedded in oil's move signal that geopolitical inflation is now feeding directly back into monetary policy.

sections.heading: ECB Set to Hold at 2.25% Thursday, With September Hike Firmly Priced In
sections.body:
The European Central Bank is widely expected to hold interest rates at 2.25% at its Thursday meeting, with interest rate swaps pricing an 87% probability of no change. But the pause masks a hawkish lean: markets assign a 75% probability of a September hike and a 34% chance of another in October.

Eurozone annual inflation fell to 2.8% in June from 3.2% in May, but the ECB's Governing Council is wrestling with an energy-driven inflation resurgence that could push headline numbers back up. A Reuters poll of economists confirmed the consensus for a July hold followed by a September hike, even if oil prices stabilize, partly because a European heatwave and fertiliser shortages threaten food price pressure.

The ECB is also considering doubling its minimum reserve requirement, a move that would withdraw approximately €160-170 billion of excess liquidity from the banking system — a form of stealth tightening that would amplify the impact of any future rate hike.

The euro sat at $1.1403, down 0.1% against the dollar, with the common currency caught between higher oil costs supporting inflation arguments and safe-haven flows into the dollar.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB is on hold but not at ease. The combination of energy inflation, a potential reserve-requirement doubling, and a September hike priced at 75% means Thursday's press conference is more about forward guidance than the rate decision itself.

sections.heading: Burnham Takes Office as Gilt Yields Rise, Sterling Dips
sections.body:
Andy Burnham became Britain's seventh prime minister in a decade on Monday, appointing former Defence Secretary John Healey as Chancellor of the Exchequer and Ed Miliband as Foreign Secretary. Burnham reiterated his commitment to the previous government's fiscal rules.

Markets responded cautiously. Benchmark British gilt yields rose and sterling dipped 0.4% to $1.3375 as investors weighed the prospect of higher government spending under a Burnham government and questioned how Healey would finance it. Barclays noted that the UK jobs market appears to have 'stabilised at weak levels', with annual wage growth and unemployment steady in the three months to May.

The IG analysis noted that gilt yields have moved sharply since Burnham's appointment, with investors demanding a premium for uncertainty about fiscal direction. Healey, a veteran defence secretary with no Treasury experience, faces immediate pressure to outline spending plans amid the broader Middle East inflation shock affecting Europe.
sections.whyItMatters: A new PM with a new chancellor and no Treasury track record is a risk premium event for gilts. With energy inflation already pressuring the BoE, any signal of fiscal expansion from Healey will tighten UK borrowing costs further.

sections.heading: China's Rate Freeze Hits 14 Months as Q2 GDP Slumps to Post-Pandemic Low
sections.body:
China's Loan Prime Rate remained frozen for a 14th consecutive month as Q2 GDP growth slipped to 4.3% — the weakest since late 2022 and well below Beijing's 4.5-5% full-year target. Retail sales grew just 1%, failing to convince markets that domestic demand has returned, while property investment fell 18% year-on-year in the first half.

The oil-price shock from the Iran conflict is blocking broad rate cuts: higher energy costs are feeding through to Chinese industrial input prices, making monetary easing politically difficult despite the growth slowdown. According to IG, the KOSPI plunged 8.8% as South Korea's chip sector sold off, with SK Hynix diving 16% and Samsung falling 11%, compounded by new curbs on leveraged ETFs.

Markets are now focused on the late-July Politburo meeting as Beijing's real policy signal for the second half. The key question is whether authorities will prioritise growth support via fiscal stimulus — or maintain discipline amid imported inflation from the Middle East conflict.

The Nigeria CBN also held its benchmark rate at 26.5% on Tuesday, with Governor Cardoso citing 'heightened global uncertainties' from Middle East hostilities. Headline inflation eased marginally to 15.91%, but food inflation pressures remain acute.
sections.whyItMatters: China's inability to cut rates despite a growth slowdown — because the Iran war is pushing up input costs — is a textbook case of geopolitical inflation trapping policymakers. The Politburo's late-July decision will signal whether Beijing prioritises growth or price stability.

bottomLine: Three distinct but reinforcing forces are reshaping the macro landscape this week. The Senate's Russia sanctions bill — now filibuster-proof — marks a new escalation in financial warfare, but its secondary tariffs on Russian energy buyers like China and India could accelerate dedollarization even as it squeezes Moscow. Trump's 50% Canada tariffs, deployed via an untested 1930s statute, open a second trade-war front that pulls North American supply chains into uncertainty while USMCA renegotiation is still underway. And with Brent above $91, the US-Iran conflict entering its 11th night, and the Houthis now threatening Saudi Red Sea shipping, both the Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb chokepoints are under simultaneous threat for the first time. The ECB holds Thursday but September is priced for a hike. China cannot cut rates despite a growth slump because the same oil shock driving ECB hawkishness is blocking Beijing's easing room. The converging pressure is unusual: geopolitical inflation feeding monetary tightening, trade fragmentation feeding supply disruption, and sanctions architecture feeding dollar-competitor alternatives.
sources: AP News, CNBC, RFE/RL, Reuters, Bloomberg, White House Fact Sheet, Nairametrics, IG Markets, LiveMint, FXEmpire, Moneycontrol, NYT, USA Today, Yahoo Finance, Nigerian CBN, Moneycontrol Commodities
